title: SAFE-GUARD: Audio Emotion Recognition System for Real-Time Public Place Abuse Detection
authors: Kanan, Rashmi Chawla, Giancarlo Fortino
abstract: Ensuring safety in privacy-sensitive environments requires unobtrusive monitoring methods that respect user dignity while remaining effective. Audio-based approaches provide a promising alternative to video surveillance but face challenges of robustness, generalization, and interpretability. To address these, we propose a multimodal Voice Distress Detection System for real-time safety monitoring. The system combines acoustic descriptors extracted with TorchAudio, speech transcripts generated by Whisper, and schema-constrained inference through a fine-tuned DialoGPT-medium model. Outputs are structured to include distress level, confidence, reasoning, and recommended action, ensuring interpretability in safety-critical contexts. Evaluation on four benchmark datasets (RAVDESS, CREMA-D, TESS, IEMOCAP) demonstrates strong performance, achieving 90.65% accuracy and 98.72% recall on distress cases. Compared to a logistic regression baseline trained on acoustic features alone, our multimodal fusion approach significantly improves sensitivity. These results highlight the potential of our framework as a privacy-preserving solution for real-time abuse detection in sensitive spaces.

title: Generative AI-driven Group Emotion Recognition: A Comparative Analysis of Holistic and Compositional Strategies
authors: Pasquale Molinaro, Mattia Presta, Giancarlo Fortino
abstract: Group Emotion Recognition (GER) represents a significant challenge in computer vision, as traditional Convolutional Neural Networks often fail to capture the semantic context required to interpret collective affective states. This paper investigates the potential of Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) applied to GER in a zero-shot setting, eliminating the need for task-specific training. We propose a comparative study between two distinct methodological pipelines: a Compositional approach, which aggregates individual facial expressions detected in the scene, and a Holistic approach, which leverages the VLM’s capability to interpret global scene context and interactions. Six state-of-the-art open-source VLMs are evaluated (LLaVA-1.6, GLM-4.1V, MiniCPM-V-4.5, InternVL3, Moondream2 and PaliGemma2) on a curated dataset derived from MELD. To overcome the limitations of standard binary classification, performance is assessed using a proposed semantic metric based on Russell’s Circumplex Model, measuring the coherence of predictions, assessing a more nuanced, sophisticated and precise evaluation. Experimental results demonstrate the distinct superiority of the Holistic approach (Subset Accuracy 86% vs. 62%). Moreover the study leverages a new methodology for GER in real-time, with a latency time near to the zero (Paligemma model inference time in ≈ 0.16 seconds)

title: Impact of Renewable Integration on Day-Ahead and Real-Time Market Prices under One- and Two-Price Imbalance Settlements
authors: Gergo Varhegyi, Mutasim Nour
abstract: This paper investigates the influence of renewable energy integration on the pricing dynamics of day-ahead and real-time markets under one- and two-price imbalance settlement methods. As the penetration of renewable energy sources increases, the accurate allocation of revenue and payments becomes critical to ensure market efficiency and participant equity. One-price settlements ensure that all imbalances are settled at a single price, typically the system marginal price, leading to revenue and payment neutrality. In contrast, two-price settlements differentiate prices based on whether the imbalance decreases or increases the overall system balance, often resulting in different financial outcomes for participants, as well as profit for the market operator. This study, powered by PLEXOS, provides a comprehensive methodology to accurately model one- and two-price imbalance settlements. The methodology combines in-software adjustments with Python-based automation to enhance data handling and reporting accuracy, enabling precise reflection of the financial impacts of renewable energy on market prices and settlements. Through a detailed test network model, the study analyses the discrepancies in market prices and allocations caused by the variability of renewable energy. The findings highlight the significant impact of renewable energy on both the day-ahead and real-time market prices, providing actionable insights for market operators and participants to optimise financial outcomes and maintain market stability. This work contributes to the broader goal of integrating renewable energy sources into electricity markets, offering practical solutions to manage price volatility and ensuring transparent and equitable market operations.

title: Mathematical Modeling of Negative Environmental Consequences Due to Thermal Radiation
authors: Yurii Skob, Oksana Pichugina, Mykola Kalinichenko
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The relevance of this work stems from the imperative to address the contemporary scientific a nd practical challenge of minimizing the risks associated with potential disruptions to the safe operation of man-made objects. This entails a comprehensive analysis of gas mixture movement in the emission zone of gas impurities, predicting air conditions in man-made environments under the influence of temperature disturbances, and the exploration of effective designs for protective equipment to mitigate or eliminate the negative consequences of such impacts. This research aims to systematically enhance and improve mathematical models, methodologies, and software for the computer implementation of a decision support system model. The model is intended to analyze the movement of a multicomponent gas mixture within the vicinity of manmade objects under the influence of t hermal <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$r$</tex> adiation. The theoretical foundation of the work is rooted in mathematical modeling methods, computational techniques in mathematical physics, probit analysis, risk assessment theories, and decisionmaking principles. The software tool developed for the computer implementation of the system model adheres to the principles of the theory of computer modeling systems. Based on the modeling results, distributions of the conditional probability of a fatal injury to a person due to exposure to thermal radiation from a high-temperature gas jet at specific control points a re derived for two design scenarios: one without protection for the personnel workplace and another with a continuous protective barrier impervious to radiation. The observed distribution patterns emphasize the crucial consideration of barriers impervious to heat flows when assessing safety at man-made facilities releasing high-temperature gases into the atmosphere.

title:
Dynamic Event-Triggered MPC for Nonlinear Systems: Application to Vehicle Active Suspensions
                    <sup>*</sup>
authors: Wenting He, Daiwen Yao, Michael Shi, Xin Yu, Ning Zhao, Huiyan Zhang, Levente Kovács
abstract: Model Predictive Control (MPC) faces the challenge of heavy online computational burden in vehicle active suspension systems. To alleviate this issue, this paper proposes a Dynamic Event-Triggered MPC (DET-MPC) method considering nonlinear actuator constraints. By introducing an internal dynamic variable to adaptively adjust the triggering threshold, this method updates the control law only when significant changes occur in the system states, thereby substantially reducing the number of optimization computations. Theoretical analysis demonstrates the uniform asymptotic stability of the closed-loop system and excludes Zeno behavior. Simulation results show that, compared with time-triggered and static event-triggered strategies, the proposed method reduces the controller update frequency by up to 82.9% and significantly lowers the CPU execution time, while maintaining comparable performance in body acceleration and suspension travel control, significantly alleviating the computational and communication burden.

title: The Impact of Quality Control, Curated Gene Sets, and Statistical Frameworks on Alzheimer’s Disease Gene Expression Analysis in Single-Cell Data
authors: Natasha Petrovska, Maria Larrondo-Petrie, Mirjana Pavlovic
abstract: Single-cell transcriptomics enables detailed investigation of Alzheimer’s disease (AD)–associated gene expression, yet findings are highly sensitive to preprocessing, gene selection, and statistical methods. Motivated by the need for reproducible detection of sex- and age-specific transcriptional patterns, we systematically evaluated the impact of quality control (QC), curated gene sets, and non-parametric analyses on human and mouse datasets. Our objective was to determine how these methodological choices influence gene-level variance, differential expression, and cross-species reproducibility. QC improved data integrity by removing low-quality cells and outliers, enhancing variance and interpretability. Comparison of three curated gene sets—GeneCards, Seligman, and Driessens—revealed that the GeneCards subset captured a balanced spectrum of AD-related variability. Sex-stratified Wilcoxon analyses identified female-biased upregulation of UCHL1, while LITAF and OPCML displayed opposite sex-biased modulation in mice versus humans. Age-dependent Kruskal–Wallis tests revealed multiple conserved genes (SQSTM1, APOE, ITM2B, MAPT, UCHL1, MT-ND1, PRNP, FUS, VCP, and BLMH) with significant changes across age groups. This study demonstrates robust sex-and age-specific signatures and highlights how QC, gene-set selection, and statistical frameworks jointly influence reproducible single-cell AD analyses.

title: A Containerized Multimodal Architecture for On-Device Driver-State Feedback with ROS 2 and Edge AI Services
authors: Raul Fernandez-Matellan, David Martin Gomez, Arturo de la Escalera Hueso
abstract: Modern automated-driving platforms increasingly rely on multimodal driver monitoring to support supervision and adaptive interaction. However, real-vehicle deployment is often hindered by dependency coupling across robotics middle-ware, sensor drivers, and heterogeneous artificial-intelligence runtimes. We validated an on-device, containerized driver-state feedback architecture that combines Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2) acquisition with ROS-free inference containers via a shared-memory (SHM) Bridge, distilling multi-camera video and wearable physiology into compact semantic messages. Vision pipelines were evaluated across heterogeneous runtimes (OpenVINO for head pose, YOLOv8 via ONNX Runtime for person/proximity, and Ultralytics YOLOv8 for driver–object interaction), together with a physiological awareness module. The loop was closed with a local large language model (LLM), served via Ollama (Llama 3.2), to generate structured on-screen and audio feedback. In a one-hour sustained benchmark, the SHM-Bridge overhead averaged 2.45 ms (median 0.21 ms), remaining small relative to inference latency (5.23 ms physiology, 39.26 ms OpenVINO, 189.40 ms ONNX, 313.74 ms Ultralytics, and 2093.05 ms LLM). These results support SHM-Bridge decoupling as a practical strategy for modular multimodal driver monitoring in real vehicles by isolating software dependencies and mitigating runtime conflicts.

title: Perception and Acceptance of Quadruped Robots Based on Indirect Exposure
authors: Veronika Egger, Layali Mashkaf, Günter Josef Hofer, Dea Hasanaj, Mathias Brandstötter
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Advanced Robotics and its Social Impacts (ARSO)
abstract: Creating social robots is a multidisciplinary challenge that requires understanding human perception, behavior interpretation, and social–ethical expectations in human–robot interaction. Animal-like robots are a key category in this domain, yet quantitative evidence on user acceptance and perceived safety, especially for quadruped robots, remains limited. We therefore conducted a multilingual survey on the perception and acceptance of quadruped robots with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{N} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{4 5 4}$</tex> participants. Importantly, participants formed their judgments from indirect exposure to standardized visual stimuli (images and short videos) rather than from direct physical interaction. The results suggest that reported comfort under indirect exposure is associated with robot appearance and the coherence of design cues across animal-like and machine-like constructions, with mismatched zoomorphic cues tending to be viewed less favorably. Concerns about job loss and accidents are negatively associated with perceived safety, while concerns about unfair decisions and privacy show no significant influence. Prior experience with robotics is associated with higher acceptance and reduced anxiety. Appearance shows up to support initial sense-making, but did not correspond to uniformly high reported trust; responsibility attribution in the event of malfunction also remained contested, with responses clustering into technology-centered, human-centered, and shared-responsibility positions. Overall, the findings provide exploratory quantitative evidence on mediated first impressions of quadruped robots and motivate follow-up studies using direct human–robot interaction to test, validate, and extend these observations.

title: Modern Trends in the Use of Hydrogen as a Motor Fuel: Analysis, Recent Advancementsand Prospects
authors: Sergii Boichenko, Iryna Shkilniuk
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Energy Smart Systems (ESS)
abstract: The global shift towards sustainable and environmentally friendly energy sources has necessitated the exploration of alternative fuels and energy carriers. Hydrogen, with its inexhaustible reserves and minimal environmental impact, stands out as a promising contender in the transportation sector. This article provides an informational and analytical review of the current state of hydrogen technologies use as motor fuel.

title: Disaster Response Gross Motor, Fine Motor and Tactile Workload Prediction Using Multi-layer Perceptrons
authors: Mark-Robin Giolando, Julie A. Adams
abstract: Human-robot teams executing disaster response missions require human workload predictions to enable future robots to preemptively adapt to adverse workload conditions. Workload estimation algorithms have demonstrated the ability to estimate the current human workload based on physiological response metrics; however, existing prediction methods for future workload values have only made mid-mission workload predictions up to 30 seconds, and only for cognitive workload. Three physical workload component (i.e., gross motor, fine motor and tactile) prediction models are presented in this work, making predictions up to 420 seconds. Pretrained workload estimation models converted physiological response signals into historical and current workload inputs to act as inputs for the forecasting models. Multi-layer perceptron models were trained to forecast gross motor, fine motor and tactile workload, with accurate predictions made for three minutes for the gross motor, and seven minutes for the fine motor and tactile. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of multi-minute workload predictions for human-robot teams executing physical tasks.

title: Automated Detection of Hip Replacements and Fiducial Markers in Pelvic CT Scans: A Comparative Study of Rule-Based vs Deep Learning Approaches
authors: Sambit Bhattacharya, Aniruddh Karunaker, Brian Anderson, Daxell Wells, Yuan Gao, Lawrence Marks, Shiva Das, Arcot Rajasekar, Lukasz Mazur
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive comparative study of automated detection of hip replacements and fiducial markers in pelvic computed tomography (CT) scans, evaluating rule-based anatomically-constrained methods against modern deep learning techniques. We develop and independently evaluate two distinct approaches: (1) a novel rule-based system utilizing metallic implant detection, fiducial marker identification, and anatomical constraint analysis specifically designed for hip replacement detection, and (2) a convolutional neural network (CNN) enhanced with Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) for hip prosthesis and fiducial detection. Our evaluation on a comprehensive dataset of 444 pelvic CT cases demonstrates that the rule-based approach achieves superior performance (sensitivity: 90.4%, specificity: 88.2%, accuracy: 89.0%) while providing interpretability and clinical reasoning. The CNN achieves 70.8% ± 5.4% accuracy with GradCAM visualizations enabling spatial understanding of predictions for hip prosthesis and fiducial detection. This comparative analysis reveals that specialized rule-based methods can significantly outperform deep learning approaches while offering the interpretability essential for clinical adoption in medical imaging applications focused on data curation and quality control.

title: Lightweight LLM Adaptation for Intrusion Detection via Token-Efficient Flow Representation
authors: Akram Laamari, Hajar Moudoud, Zakaria Abou El Houda
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are emerging as a promising approach for intrusion detection using structured network flow data. However, their practical deployment is constrained by context window limitations and the excessive token overhead introduced by conventional tabular serialization formats such as JSON. Verbose data representations inflate sequence lengths, often exceeding model input limits and causing feature truncation. Additionally, it remains unclear which LLM architecture is more suitable for structured intrusion detection tasks under limited training resources. To tackle this issue, we propose a novel representation-aware intrusion detection framework based on Token-Oriented Object Notation (TOON), a compact serialization format that maximizes token efficiency while preserving schema structure. Also, we integrate a Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) scheme to enable parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Finally, we evaluate the proposed framework as an encoder–decoder model (T5-Small) with a decoder-only model (Qwen2.5) on three benchmark datasets, including NSL-KDD, UNSW-NB15, and CIC-IDS2018 for binary and multi-class classification scenarios. The results show that our tokenizer-aligned, representation-aware preprocessing combined with lightweight encoder–decoder adaptation provides a practical and resource-efficient foundation for LLM-based intrusion detection.

title: Intelligent Safe Optimal Control Towards Koopman Operator-Driven Nonlinear Systems with Asymmetric State and Input Constraints
authors: Yalu Su, Ding Wang, Mingming Zhao, Dan Xiong, Yiyong Huang, Wei Han
abstract: For unknown nonlinear systems subject to asymmetric state and input constraints simultaneously, this article establishes a safe value iteration paradigm to learn an optimal control policy in a data-based manner. Initially, the Koopman operator, instead of the black-box neural network, is applied to extract the inherent dynamics of the controlled systems from the measured data, thereby allowing for explicit analysis of the prediction error. To tackle the issue posed by state and input constraints, a crafted control barrier function is seamlessly incorporated into the canonical utility function, which retains the property of positive definiteness for the asymmetric case. Moreover, the value iteration algorithm with regard to the augmented utility function is adopted to attain a safe optimal controller, where the actor and critic networks are leveraged to approximate the control input and associated value function, respectively. The mono-tonicity, safety, and stability of the raised algorithm are further verified rigorously. Via performing three experiments on the linear system, the nonlinear system, and the manipulator plant, comparative results are obtained to substantiate the superiority and efficacy of the developed approach in achieving optimal performance and safe guarantee.

title: From Episodic Screening to Continuous Insight: AI Architectures for Colorectal Care
authors: Tejas Padliya
abstract: Integrating wearables and clinical data to reshape colorectal cancer (CRC) prevention.Colorectal cancer (CRC) pathways are still dominated by episodic screening through colonoscopy and stool-based tests, despite growing access to consumer sensors and mobile computing. This article presents an end-to-end AI architecture for multimodal colorectal risk stratification that combines traditional screening data with passively collected activity patterns, heart rate variability, stool frequency logs, and nutrition context. We describe a layered design consisting of data ingestion, feature engineering, temporal modeling, risk scoring, and clinician-facing decision support. Implementation patterns are illustrated using cloud-native and edge components suitable for deployment in both high-resource and resource-constrained health systems. The article discusses issues such as bias, data sparsity, longitudinal drift, and integration with existing screening guidelines. As a result, it provides a reference model that future clinical and engineering teams can adapt when building continuous, AI-assisted colorectal screening and monitoring tools.

title: Fuzzy Logic Optimization of Electrode Geometry in a Microfluidic Device for Malaria Cell Separation
authors: Jenil Patel, Rushvi Patel, Mahesh Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: Current work demonstrates numerical investigation of a microfluidic cell sorting device for separation of malaria infected cells from healthy red blood cells using dielectrophoresis (DEP) phenomenon. In present study, a microfluidic device microchannel was designed using finite element method-based simulations to examine the impact of electrode geometry concerning its height and width on cell separation trajectories. The cell separation performance was evaluated based on four performance parameters which include red blood cell purity, malaria cell purity, red blood cell yield, and malaria cell yield and used to model a fuzzy inference system model implemented in MATLAB to determine suitable geometrical dimension of electrode geometry for desired performance parameters. The results have shown that there is a direct impact of electrode dimension variation on electric field distribution and further on dielectrophoretic force acting on the cells. The optimized geometry can provide high purity and yield of malaria –infected cells. Results have shown that ~100% blood cell purity and ~100% malaria cell yield has been achieved at 3V and 100 kHz with effectively focused cells and aligned cell trajectories before entering in DEP cell separation effects.

title: HeuRiskUNet: UAV Navigation in Risk Maps through Improved Path Planning Heuristics
authors: E. Aldao, F. Veiga-López, C. P. C. Chanel, Y. Watanabe, H. González-Jorge
abstract: In recent years, UAVs have been increasingly deployed across various applications, including environmental monitoring, infrastructure inspection, and logistics. While many of these operations are still conducted by human remote pilots, the current trend is towards the development of fully autonomous solutions, which require efficient and robust control and path-planning systems. Traditionally, the most widely used path-planning algorithm is A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">*</sup> due to its deterministic behaviour and computational efficiency. This approach discretises the navigation environment into a grid with adjacent-node connectivity, thus constructing a graph, and minimises the path length through a graph search guided by heuristic functions. Nevertheless, the applicability of this approach is often limited to shortest-path problems where admissible heuristic functions are available. In other optimisation scenarios, such as when accounting for factors like operational risk, identifying heuristics that are both admissible and capable of efficiently guiding the search can be highly challenging. Another drawback of A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">*</sup> approaches lies in their discretisation, which restricts UAV movement to adjacent nodes. This limitation reduces vehicle manoeuvrability and compromises both path smoothness and optimality. To address these challenges, this paper presents a system based on machine-learning based heuristics for navigation within complex risk maps. The system is integrated into a customised A<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">*</sup> framework that employs a k-Non-Aligned Nearest Neighbours (k-NANN) approach to compute smooth and optimal paths while maintaining low computational cost, deterministic behaviour, and interpretability.

title: SKYWALKER: Dual-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Autonomous Crawling in a Planar Microgravity Analog : Sim-to-Real Evaluation at ESA’s Orbital Robotics Lab
authors: Dadi H. Davidsson, Bruno M. De Salas, Glenn G. Svendsen, Rasmus D. Kristiansen, Gustav Hansen, Artem Melnyk, Alaa E. Kawara, Thor Nielsen, Jon Argjaboda, Simon Bøgh, Anton B. Mortensen
abstract: SKYWALKER is a dual-agent reinforcement learning framework that enables a free-floating robotic platform to "crawl" hand-over-hand between fixed anchor points in a planar microgravity analog. The task is divided between two cooperating RL agents, one for arm-based anchoring and one for base repositioning. Both agents are trained in a frictionless simulation environment.In hardware trials conducted at the European Space Agency’s Orbital Robotics Lab (ORL) as part of the ESA Academy Experiments Programme, the learned arm policy (Agent A) successfully performed autonomous docking, while the base-motion policy (Agent B) encountered sim-to-real transfer issues and was temporarily replaced with a scripted fallback. Subsequently, Agent B was refined and validated on a physical ball-bearing testbed at Aalborg University, enabling fully learned anchor-to-anchor crawling.These results demonstrate the feasibility of learning-based control for on-orbit assembly tasks and highlight the critical role of high-fidelity simulation and robust safety measures in deploying reinforcement learning for space robotics.

title: Synthesize, Adapt, Steal: A Few-Shot Domain Adaptive Model Stealing Attack for Tabular Data
authors: Maxime Schwarzer, Gustavo Sánchez, Thies Möhlenhof, Laurin Holz, Johannes F. Loevenich, Roberto Rigolin F. Lopes, Veit Hagenmeyer
abstract: The growing popularity of Machine Learning as a Service (MLaaS) is creating new attack vectors. Model theft poses a particular threat because it enables Intellectual Property (IP) to be stolen and can serve as a starting point for further attacks. While attacks on computer vision (CV) are well-studied, tabular data crucial for industrial applications remains less explored. We present a few-shot, domain-adaptive, model-stealing attack optimized specifically for tabular data. Our approach combines three components: (1) generating a synthetic yet plausible data pool using a Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CTGAN); (2) a hybrid query strategy combining active learning with adversarial augmentation to efficiently exploit the query budget; and (3) a novel Relational Attention Corrector (RAC) that implicitly learns and enforces semantic validity. Experiments on an energy critical infrastructure intrusion detection dataset (specifically, the ERENO IEC 61850 dataset) and standard benchmarks (Adult Income and Diabetes) demonstrate that our attack significantly outperforms previous methods. It improves the macro F1-score by over 24 percentage points with only 7,000 queries, closely approaching the performance of an attacker with full data knowledge.

title: Efficient Disruption of Criminal Networks through Multi-Objective Genetic Algorithms
authors: Yehezkiel Darmadi, Thanh Thi Nguyen, Campbell Wilson
abstract: Criminal networks, such as the Sicilian Mafia, pose substantial threats to public safety, national security, and economic stability. Outdated disruption methods with a focus on removing influential individuals or key players have proven ineffective due to the covertness of the network. Thus, researchers have been trying to apply Social Network Analysis (SNA) techniques, such as centrality-based measures, to identify key players. However, removing individuals with high centrality often proves to be inefficient, as it does not mimic the real-world scenarios that Law Enforcement Agencies (LEAs) face. For instance, the operational costs limit the LEAs from exploiting the results of the centrality-based methods. This study proposes a multi-objective optimisation framework like the Weighted Sum Genetic Algorithm (WS-GA) and the Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II (NSGA-II) to identify disruption strategies that balance two conflicting goals, maximising fragmentation and minimising operational cost which is captured by the spatial distance between nodes and the nearest LEA headquarters. The study utilises the "Montagna Operation" dataset for the experiments. The results demonstrate that although centrality-based approaches can fragment network effectively, they tend to incur higher operational costs. In contrast, the proposed algorithms achieve comparable disruption outcomes with significantly lower operational costs. The contribution of this work lies in incorporating operational costs in a form of spatial distance constraints into disruption strategy, which has been largely overlooked in prior studies. This research offers a scalable multi-objective capability that improves practical application of SNA in guiding LEAs in disrupting criminal networks more efficiently and strategically.

title: Design and Simulation of a Battery Electric Vehicle Model for Underground Mining Applications with Synthetic Data Generation for Machine Learning Model
authors: Tamsa, Arpit Garg, Wen L. Soong, S. Ali Pourmousavi
abstract: The shift to electrifying underground mining operations poses considerable challenges in accurately predicting energy consumption due to the intricate nature of operations and the scarcity of real-world performance data for mining electric trucks. This paper introduces a comprehensive dataset synthesis framework as well as the application of four machine learning techniques aimed at forecasting energy usage in electric mining trucks used underground. This addresses the essential need for data-driven optimisation tools in sustainable mining practices. Four machine learning models, including decision tree, random forest, extreme gradient boosting, and deep neural network, were thoroughly assessed using the synthetic dataset comprising 50,000 operational scenarios created through physics-based modelling involving 11 operational parameters. Extensive hyperparameter tuning was conducted using 5-fold cross-validation with a focus on minimising mean absolute error. The Deep Neural Network exhibited outstanding predictive performance (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.9998, RMSE = 1.02 kWh, MAE = 0.60 kWh), showcasing strong generalisation across various operational scenarios. Model validation against physics-based calculations for a typical haul profile confirmed prediction accuracy. This framework provides mining operators with reliable tools to manage energy-efficient fleets, optimise routes, and plan operations.

title: LLM-Augmented Feature Engineering for Machine Learning Pipelines
authors: A. Rancea, I. Anghel, T. Cioara
abstract: Feature engineering represents a fundamental challenge in machine learning (ML) pipelines for extracting meaningful representations that capture underlying patterns, starting from raw data. Usually the process requires manual, time consuming, deep domain expertise, and is difficult to be scaled across multiple domains. Automatic methods attempt to address these challenges by generating predefined statistical features or selecting optimal subsets from a feature set, rather than creating new ones. In this paper, we propose a Large Language Model (LLM)-based automated feature engineering pipeline using Mistral-7B-Instruct with Retrieval-Augmented Generation and a multi-criteria validation process. It starts with prompt text and additionally integrates domain and data knowledge, to create and validate new features. We evaluate the solution in the context of an ML-based heart rate prediction process and show that the generated features achieve 9-48% MSE improvements across three different time series architectures (LSTM, Transformer, and Informer). The results demonstrate that LLM-based feature generation can significantly optimize and improve ML prediction tasks.

title: Privacy-Preserving and Coercion-Resistant E-Voting via Unlinkable Biometric Credentials on a Hybrid Permissioned Blockchain
authors: Kumar Shubhranshu, Harsh Kumar Verma
publish: 2026 IEEE 4th International Conference on Device Intelligence, Computing and Communication Technologies (DICCT)
abstract: The research presents a secure voting system which enables verification of all electoral processes through its complete verification mechanisms at the ballot box using fingerprint authentication and permissioned blockchain technology to issue anonymous credentials. The proposed system enables local eligibility authentication through its biometric data protection which does not require biometric template storage in its on-chain system. Voters use the blind signature system to obtain credentials which allow them to send encrypted votes with zero-knowledge Aproofs that verify their ballot’s authenticity while keeping their voting choice hidden. The system records votes onto a Byzantine fault-tolerant ledger which uses threshold decryption to calculate final results. The system establishes auditability through its Merkle root system which periodically registers ballot Merkle roots on the public chain. The system maintains voter privacy through its revoting feature which enables voters to cast new votes while the system handles large election events. The experimental tests performed on a Hyperledger Besu system with ten nodes showed that the system maintained a throughput rate above 1600 TPS while it achieved confirmation times below one second during the controlled testing input conditions.
